metadata.working_title: A multiscale computational framework linking biochemical pattern formation to tissue-level spine morphogenesis in molluscan shells

abstract_sketch.problem: Connecting molecular-scale biochemical processes to organism-level morphological variation in non-model organisms remains a core challenge in developmental biology.
abstract_sketch.gap: Existing models of shell pattern formation operate either at the biochemical scale (pigmentation) or at the tissue scale (mechanical buckling), but rarely bridge both in a unified framework that maps to empirical phenotypes.
abstract_sketch.approach: We propose a modular multiscale framework that couples a two-species Activator-Substrate reaction-diffusion model at the molecular level to a morphoelastic rod model of mantle deformation at the tissue level, with a Quality-Diversity (MAP-Elites) algorithm that builds a look-up table from molecular parameter sets to tissue-level growth profiles. Spine images from real shells are matched to this look-up table to infer population-level parameter distributions.
abstract_sketch.key_results: Applied to three populations of the gastropod Turbo sazae in Japan (Hayama, Jyogashima, Tateyama; n=12, 16, 9 shells respectively), the framework reveals a statistically significant population-level difference in the tissue-level growth rate g1: Jyogashima shells have g1 values on average 0.25 higher than Hayama (p = 1.202e-5) and 0.4 higher than Tateyama (p = 5.7e-6) by two-sided Wilcoxon rank-sum tests. The corresponding biochemical parameters c2 and c3 also shift between populations, consistent with a molecular-level mechanism.
abstract_sketch.impact: The framework is modular and plug-and-play at each scale, opening a path for linking environmental, biochemical, and mechanical drivers of morphological variation in other non-model organisms.

sections.title: Introduction
content_bullets: Connecting across scales is a central challenge in computational biology
Molluscan shells are a canonical study system for growth and pattern formation
Existing tissue-mechanical models require a pre-pattern whose origin is unspecified
Biochemical models exist but have not been coupled to tissue mechanics
Turbo sazae displays phenotypic plasticity in spine form
We build a multiscale framework and apply it to three T. sazae populations

sections.title: Background and Related Work
content_bullets: Theoretical morphology of shells from Raup onward
Mechanical models of shell ornamentation and buckling
Reaction-diffusion and neural models of shell pigmentation
Quality-Diversity algorithms and MAP-Elites
Population-level morphological variation in molluscs

subsections.title: Molecular-level Activator-Substrate model
content_bullets: Two-species reaction-diffusion with six parameters {c1,c2,c3,c4,d1,d2}
Autocatalytic activator with substrate consumption
Fixed diffusion coefficients

subsections.title: Tissue-level morphoelastic rod model
content_bullets: Planar elastic rod with moment-curvature constitutive law m=Eb u
Shell edge forcing f=k(p-r) and calcification evolution
Gaussian growth profile parameterized by g1 (height) and g2 (width)
Bending stiffness linked to growth

subsections.title: Quality-Diversity exploration via MAP-Elites
content_bullets: Use qdpy implementation
Pre-integrate tissue model across (g1,g2) pairs to build a zoology
Map molecular Gaussian fit quality to a grid indexed by (g1,g2)

subsections.title: Shell collection and parameter extraction
content_bullets: Specimens collected from Najima in Hayama, Jyogashima in Miura, Okinoshima in Tateyama
n=12 Hayama, n=16 Jyogashima, n=9 Tateyama
Digital and dissection-microscope photography
Mathematica-based GUI overlay fitting of {g1,g2}

subsections.title: Growth parameters shape the spine morphospace
content_bullets: Higher g1 yields more curved spines with earlier self-contact
Higher g2 yields wider spines
Lower g2 produces narrower spine tip

subsections.title: Biochemical parameters map onto the growth morphospace
content_bullets: c1, c2, c3 cluster in distinct (g1,g2) regions
c4 largely unconstrained
Upper-right boundary of morphospace matches linear stability analysis

subsections.title: Population-level differences in spine form
content_bullets: Jyogashima g1 distribution is 0.25 higher than Hayama (p=1.202e-5) and 0.4 higher than Tateyama (p=5.7e-6)
Hayama vs Tateyama g1 not significantly different
g2 differences small
Jyogashima shows higher average c2 and lower c3

sections.title: Discussion
content_bullets: Interpretation of biochemical-to-tissue differences
Possible environmental link via food availability and wave exposure
Limitations: phenomenological biochemical model, single-spine focus
Plug-and-play extensibility to other systems and more realistic molecular models
Future directions: whole-shell modelling, transcriptomics

table_plan.title: Sample sizes and statistical comparison of g1 distributions across populations
table_plan.caption_sketch: Sample sizes per population and two-sided Wilcoxon rank-sum test of g1 differences relative to Jyogashima.

table_plan.title: Tissue- and molecular-level parameters
table_plan.caption_sketch: Summary of model parameters at the molecular and tissue scales.

micro_queries: Chirat mantle buckling spine molluscan shell
single-cell RNA sequencing mantle transcriptome mollusc
must_cite: Chirat et al. spine formation buckling

figure_plan.caption: Schematic of the multiscale framework: molecular Activator-Substrate model, tissue-level morphoelastic rod, and the Quality-Diversity look-up table connecting them.
Qualitative morphospace of spine shape as a function of the Gaussian growth parameters g1 (rate) and g2 (width).
Population-level differences in g1. Mean shift and p-values from two-sided Wilcoxon rank-sum tests.
Shell sample sizes collected per population (n=12, 16, 9).
